content.full_text:
Legislative Counsel
of California

GEORGE H. MURPHY

Sacramento, California
May 10, 1976

Mr. A. Alan Post
Legislative Analyst
925 L Street, Suite 650
Sacramento, CA 95814

Campaign and Disclosure: Audits - #7505

Dear Mr. Post:

You have asked the three questions separately
stated and considered below regarding the auditing of
statements and reports required to be filed pursuant to
the Political Reform Act of 1974 (Title 9 (commencing
with Section 81000), Gov. C.).¹

QUESTION NO. 1

Who has authority to establish auditing standards
for the auditing of campaign and lobbying statements and
reports required to be filed with the Secretary of State
pursuant to the Political Reform Act of 1974?

OPINION NO. 1

The Fair Political Practices Commission has primary authority to establish such standards pursuant to its
rule-making power. In addition, the Franchise Tax Board
has authority to establish auditing standards which are
reasonably necessary to carry out its statutory duties.
Such standards must be consistent with any regulations
adopted by the Fair Political Practices Commission.

¹ All section references are to the Government Code,
unless otherwise indicated.

I can see this image appears to be a mostly blank page with a few small marks or holes at the top, but I cannot discern any legible text content to transcribe. The page appears to be either blank or the text is too faint or unclear to read accurately.

Mr. A. Alan Post - p. 2 - #7505

ANALYSIS NO. 1

The Political Reform Act of 1974 imposes a statutory duty on the Franchise Tax Board to make audits of statements and reports filed with the Secretary of State pursuant to the campaign and lobbying disclosure provisions of the act (Sec. 90000). The board is required to audit the statements and reports of each registered lobbyist and specified candidates and committees (Sec. 90001). Instead of the board, the Fair Political Practices Commission is required to audit the statements of candidates for Controller and members of the Board of Equalization and committees supporting such candidates (Sec. 90006). In addition, both the board and the commission are authorized to make audits of any statement or report required by the campaign and lobbying disclosure provisions of the act (Sec. 90003).

The Franchise Tax Board is required to prepare periodically reports which contain in detail the board's findings with respect to the accuracy and completeness of each statement or report reviewed and its findings with respect to any statement or report that should have been but was not filed (Sec. 90004). It is also required to determine and report whether there is any probable cause to believe that any person or organization exceeded any of the campaign expenditure limitations of the act (Sec. 90002 (c)).

The Fair Political Practices Commission has primary responsibility for the impartial, effective administration and implementation of the Political Reform Act of 1974 (Sec. 83111). The commission is authorized to adopt rules and regulations to carry out the purposes and provisions of the act, including the auditing provisions of Chapter 10 (commencing with Section 90000) (Sec. 83112). Thus, the primary authority to establish auditing standards for the auditing of campaign and lobbying statements and reports filed with the Secretary of State rests with the commission.

However, since the statutory duty of conducting mandatory audits of statements and reports is imposed on the Franchise Tax Board, we think the board may also establish auditing standards for the audits of those statements and reports which the board is required or authorized to conduct. We understand that the board has developed such standards. Although not controlling, the contemporaneous administrative construction of a law by those charged with

The image appears to be a blank or nearly blank page with only some very faint marks or dots visible in the upper portion. The page is predominantly white/empty with minimal text content visible.

Mr. A. Alan Post - p. 3 - #7505


its enforcement and interpretation is entitled to great
weight. Generally, the courts will not depart from such
construction unless it is clearly erroneous or unauthorized
(Coca-Cola Co. v. State Bd. of Equalization, 25 Cal. 2d 918,
921).

Although the establishment of such standards is
not expressly authorized by statute, we think such establish-
ment is reasonably, if not absolutely, necessary to carry out
the board's statutory duties. Accordingly, we think the
authority to establish audit standards is implicit in the
statement of the board's statutory duties. We think the
application of such standards would be upheld by a court
if the standards are consistent with both the provisions of
the Political Reform Act of 1974 and any regulations adopted
by the Fair Political Practices Commission pursuant to
Section 83112.

QUESTION NO. 2

Is the Franchise Tax Board required to audit each
reported transaction of each person or entity subject to
mandatory audit under the Political Reform Act of 1974?

OPINION NO. 2

The Franchise Tax Board is not required to audit
each reported transaction of each person or entity subject
to a mandatory audit under the act.

ANALYSIS NO. 2

As discussed in Analysis No. 1, the act requires
the Franchise Tax Board to audit each statement and report
of each registered lobbyist and each candidate or committee
which meets specified criteria (Secs. 90001 and 90002). The
board must report in detail its findings with respect to the
accuracy and completeness of each statement and report (Secs.
90004) and determine whether there is any probable
cause to believe that any person or entity exceeded any of
the campaign expenditure limitations of the act (Sec. 90002(c)).
However, no provision of the act nor any regulation adopted
by the Fair Political Practices Commission (Title 2 (commencing
with Sec. 18000), Cal. Adm. C.) requires the board to audit
each reported transaction of each person or entity subject
to a mandatory audit under the act.

Mr. A. Alan Post - p. 4 - #7505


     We think the Franchise Tax Board may satisfy its
auditing and reporting duties under the act by the use of
random sampling or other generally accepted auditing prac-
tices and standards, so long as such practices and standards
are applied to each statement and report subject to a mandatory
audit.  The use of such practices and standards would be
consistent with the rule of statutory construction which
requires that statutes be given a reasonable and common
sense construction (Samarkand of Santa Barbara, Inc. v.
County of Santa Barbara, 216 Cal. App. 2d 341, 362; Metropolitan
Water Dist. v. Adams, 32 Cal. 2d 620, 630-631).

     Moreover, since an audit of each reported trans-
action of each person or entity would require a substantial
expenditure of public funds, we think a construction of the
auditing provisions of the act requiring such an audit would
be unreasonable and, therefore, inconsistent with the act.
However, where initial examination of a particular statement
discloses material omissions or inconsistencies or arouses
serious suspicions, it may become necessary for the board to
audit each transaction reported in the particular statement
in order to complete a reliable audit of the statement.

                         QUESTION NO. 3

     May the Franchise Tax Board conduct the audits
required by the Political Reform Act of 1974 in accordance
with the standards of evidence set forth in Chapter 5 of the
United States General Accounting Office's Standards for
Audit of Governmental Organizations, Programs, Activities
and Functions?

                    OPINION AND ANALYSIS NO. 3

     After reviewing the specified standards and the
provisions and regulations of the Political Reform Act of
1974, we conclude that the Franchise Tax Board may conduct
audits in accordance with such standards in order to comply
with its statutory duties under the act.  We think that the
use of such standards would enable the board to report in
detail its findings with respect to the accuracy and com-
pleteness of each statement and report reviewed as required
by Section 90004.  We note that the standards provide that
an audit does not require elaboration documentation to support
noncontroversial or insignificant points, but where reason

I can see this image is almost entirely blank/white with only a few very faint marks visible at the top that are not clearly legible. The image does not contain readable text that can be accurately transcribed.

Mr. A. Alan Post - p. 5 - #7505


to doubt the validity or completeness of evidence arises,
the auditor should take additional measures to authenticate
the evidence (pages 35 and 36).

Very truly yours,

George H. Murphy
Legislative Counsel

[signature]
By
Michael J. Kersten
Deputy Legislative Counsel

MJK:pfb

[The image appears to be mostly blank/white with only some faint marks at the top that are too unclear to transcribe with certainty]

May the Franchise Tax Board conduct the audits required by the Political Reform Act of 1974 in accordance with the standards of evidence set forth in Chapter 5 of the United States General Accounting Office's Standards for Audit of Governmental Organizations, Programs, Activities and Functions?

OPINION AND ANALYSIS NO. 3
